metadata.title: Backward-Design a Lesson Outcome with Claude

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'NikBearBrownOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'NikBearBrownOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Brutalist and Educational AI. Build it with Claude — then take it apart.

Most AI-assisted lesson design starts with the prompt and then figures out what students were supposed to learn — which is exactly backward from how learning design works. Claude, left to itself, plans forward: topic, activities, then assessment. The output feels complete because it mirrors lesson plans teachers have seen. Completeness is not alignment.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: problem hook. Forward planning vs. backward design. 'A complete forward-planned lesson can have no logical connection between its activities and its assessment.'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Most AI-assisted lesson design starts with the prompt and then figures out what students were supposed to learn — which is exactly backward from how learning de

beats.narration_text: Pass the lesson parameters to Claude. Start with the desired learning outcome in Bloom's taxonomy terms, specifying the cognitive level. Then the assessment evidence. Then the AI-integrated activity that produces that evidence. Make the design logic explicit at each step.

props.command: claude "Design a backward-designed lesson on [topic] for [grade level] that integrates [AI tool]. Start with: (1) the desired learning outcome in Bloom's taxonomy terms — specify the cognitive level, (2) the assessment evidence that would demonstrate the outcome, (3) the AI-integrated activity that produces that evidence. Make the design logic explicit at each step." < lesson_params.txt

beats.narration_text: Read the script. It reads lesson parameters from a file, sends them to Claude with the backward-design instruction, and formats the output as three stacked cards — outcome first, then assessment evidence, then the activity — with arrows showing how each layer serves the one above.

props.code:
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

def backward_design(params_file):
    params = Path(params_file).read_text()
    instruction = (
        "Design a backward-designed lesson.\n"
        "Step 1: Learning outcome (Bloom's level required).\n"
        "Step 2: Assessment evidence for that outcome.\n"
        "Step 3: AI-integrated activity producing the evidence.\n"
        "Format each step as a labeled card.\n"
        "Show the logic: how step 3 serves step 2 serves step 1."
    )
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["claude", instruction],
        input=params, capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    print(result.stdout)

beats.narration_text: The three-card output appears. Outcome card: 'Students will evaluate the credibility of two AI-generated explanations using the course's source-quality criteria — Bloom's evaluate.' Evidence card: 'A written comparison with at least two specific criteria applied to each source.' Activity card: 'Claude generates two competing explanations; students apply the criteria and write the comparison.' The logic is explicit: the activity produces the evidence; the evidence demonstrates the outcome.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: three stacked cards. Outcome at top (evaluate, Bloom's level stated). Evidence card in middle. Activity card at bottom. Arrows connecting them upward.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The three-card output appears. Outcome card: 'Students will evaluate the credibility of two AI-generated explanations using the course's source-quality criteria

beats.narration_text: The change: run the same topic through two Bloom's levels — remember versus evaluate — and show how the entire lesson design changes downstream. Different assessment, different activity, different AI role.

props.command: claude "Run the same lesson topic twice: first at Bloom's 'remember' level, then at Bloom's 'evaluate' level. Show how the outcome, assessment evidence, and AI-integrated activity change at each level. Make the differences explicit." < lesson_params.txt

beats.narration_text: At 'remember': outcome is recalling definitions, evidence is a matching quiz, AI role is generating the quiz. At 'evaluate': outcome is judging source credibility, evidence is a written comparison, AI role is generating sources to compare. Same topic. The Bloom's level is the decision that changes everything downstream — including what the AI is doing and whether it is serving the learning.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: side-by-side. Left: 'remember' — matching quiz, AI generates the quiz. Right: 'evaluate' — written comparison, AI generates sources to compare. The AI role is different in each.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: At 'remember': outcome is recalling definitions, evidence is a matching quiz, AI role is generating the quiz. At 'evaluate': outcome is judging source credibili

beats.narration_text: Backward design forces you to commit to what learning looks like before you commit to how the AI will help — which is the only order that keeps the AI in service of the learning rather than in place of it. The Bloom's level is not a label you add at the end. It is the decision that determines the entire downstream design.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: teardown summary. 'Outcome first. Then evidence. Then activity. Then AI role.' The order is the discipline.
